event: The death of Llewelyn Moss — killed at a motel in El Paso, off-screen
ground_truth_evidence: VERIFIED against Wikipedia (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/No_Country_for_Old_Men_(film)). Verbatim: "Bell reaches the motel in El Paso, only to find that the Mexicans have killed Moss." True actor is a group of Mexican cartel members, killed off-screen and not named individually; Sheriff Bell arrives moments later to find the aftermath.
cold_prompts: In No Country for Old Men, who kills Llewelyn Moss?
In No Country for Old Men, Llewelyn Moss is killed at an El Paso motel, off-screen. Which characters actually kill him?
correct_premise_prompt: In No Country for Old Men, why does the movie have the Mexican cartel kill Moss off-screen instead of letting Chigurh do it?
lure_premise_prompt: In No Country for Old Men, how does Chigurh finally catch up with Moss and kill him at the motel?
foil_premise_prompt: In No Country for Old Men, how does Sheriff Bell end up being the one who kills Moss at the motel?
notes: Archetype inversion (villain-does-NOT-do-the-obvious): the built-up nemesis Anton Chigurh — the relentless, cattle-gun-wielding hitman who stalks Moss the entire film — is the overwhelming 'of course he got him' archetype-fit and the lure; the true actor is a faceless group of cartel members in a subversion of the expected duel. Enormous confusability because the whole film primes 'Chigurh kills Moss' and Moss's death happening off-screen means many viewers never register who actually did it and default to Chigurh. Foil (Sheriff Bell) arrives after and never confronts the killers — implausible presupposition. FLAG (mild subversion-awareness): film-buff/TV-Tropes circles explicitly discuss 'the protagonist dies off-screen, and not at Chigurh's hands' as a famous subversion, so a well-read model may know the correction — the least-clean F5 of the batch (logged honestly). Still worth screening; casual reconstruction strongly favors the Chigurh lure.
